entries.statement: Ability to distinguish between problems that require machine learning and problems that can be solved more simply and reliably with rule-based logic or statistical methods.
Ability to evaluate build-versus-buy decisions for ML platform components with clear criteria based on organizational maturity, team capability, and long-term operational cost.
Ability to apply consistent governance standards across heterogeneous AI systems — from simple rule-based automation to large language models — without treating all systems identically.
Ability to select the appropriate mathematical and statistical methods for a given ML problem, reasoning about the assumptions, limitations, and trade-offs of each approach in context.
Ability to recognize meaningful patterns, structures, and anomalies in data prior to formal analysis, guiding hypothesis formation and methodology selection through intuitive data understanding.

Ability to interpret model outputs in business context, including understanding what a model is and is not telling you, assessing the practical significance of results, and identifying when model behavior signals a problem versus expected variation.

Ability to assess the applicability and limitations of AI tools and platforms to specific organizational situations, matching technical capabilities to problem requirements and operational constraints.

Ability to translate business requirements into AI system specifications, decomposing organizational needs into data requirements, model requirements, and evaluation criteria that are technically actionable.

Ability to perform cost-benefit reasoning for AI investments, weighing development cost, operational complexity, organizational readiness, and expected business value to recommend proportionate AI approaches.
Ability to balance competing AI system requirements — including performance, safety, fairness, transparency, and cost — making principled design trade-offs when requirements conflict.

Ability to integrate trustworthy AI principles into technical decision-making, holding validity, reliability, safety, security, resilience, accountability, transparency, explainability, privacy, and fairness as simultaneous design constraints.

Ability to bridge technical AI capability and organizational strategy, translating between what AI systems can do and what the organization needs to accomplish, at the appropriate level of abstraction for each audience.
Ability to evaluate whether AI development processes conform to established standards, policies, and regulatory requirements, identifying deviations and assessing their risk implications.

Ability to design and reason about automated AI operations, anticipating failure modes in automated pipelines, planning for graceful degradation, and maintaining reliability in systems that operate without continuous human oversight.

Ability to assess how users actually interact with AI systems in practice, recognizing gaps between intended use and actual use, identifying trust calibration issues, and anticipating misuse or over-reliance patterns.
